coredata.dc:title: Reactable ionic liquid assisted synthesis of BiPO4 and the influences of solvent on structure, morphology and photocatalytic performance
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Jun
Xia, Jiexiang
Di, Jun
Ji, Mengxia
Li, Hongping
Xu, Hui
Zhang, Qi
Lu, Jian
coredata.dc:description: Bismuth phosphate (BiPO4) has been researched as one of the important bismuth-containing salts with high-efficient photocatalytic activity under UV light irradiation. However, the morphologies and microstructures of BiPO4 photocatalyst on photocatalytic activity have not been clearly investigated. Herein, BiPO4 photocatalyst with various morphologies and microstructures have been synthesized via a facile reactable ionic liquid [Omim]H2PO4 assisted solvothermal process in H2O, ethanol, ethylene glycol (EG) and ethylene glycol monomethyl ether (EGME), respectively. The photocatalytic ability of the as-prepared samples was evaluated using ciprofloxacin (CIP) as target pollutant. The result implied that different photocatalytic activities for CIP degradation is mainly attributed to different morphologies and microstructures of BiPO4 samples prepared in different solvents. The BiPO4 sample which was prepared under H2O condition showed the highest photocatalytic activity. In addition, inorganic salt NaH2PO4·2H2O was utilized to prepare BiPO4 samples instead of [Omim]H2PO4. It can be found that the photocatalytic efficiency of BiPO4 prepared via ionic liquid exhibited higher photocatalytic activity than that of inorganic salt for the degradation of CIP under UV light irradiation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: BiPO4
Ionic liquids
Photocatalytic
UV light irradiation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD pattern of BiPO4 synthesized in different solvents, (a) H2O, (b) ethanol, (c) EG and (d) EGME.

TEM images of BiPO4 samples synthesized at 160°C in the presence of ionic liquid [Omim]H2PO4 and different solutions. H2O (a and b), ethanol (c and d), EG (e and f), EGME (g) respectively, (h) EDS spectrum of BiPO4 sample prepared in H2O solution.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of pure BiPO4 photocatalyst in the different solvents, (b) (aEphoton)1/2 vs. Ephoton curves of the as-prepared samples.
Degradation rate-time curves of CIP solution of BiPO4 prepared in ionic liquid (a) and inorganic salt NaH2PO4·2H2O (b), respectively, under UV light irradiation.
COD value of the treated CIP solution for different photocatalysts under ultraviolet light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Reactable ionic liquid assisted synthesis of BiPO4 and the influences of solvent on structure, morphology and photocatalytic performance

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute for Energy Research, Jiangsu University, 301 Xuefu Road, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bismuth phosphate (BiPO4) has been researched as one of the important bismuth-containing salts with high-efficient photocatalytic activity under UV light irradiation. However, the morphologies and microstructures of BiPO4 photocatalyst on photocatalytic activity have not been clearly investigated. Herein, BiPO4 photocatalyst with various morphologies and microstructures have been synthesized via a facile reactable ionic liquid [Omim]H2PO4 assisted solvothermal process in H2O, ethanol, ethylene glycol (EG) and ethylene glycol monomethyl ether (EGME), respectively. The photocatalytic ability of the as-prepared samples was evaluated using ciprofloxacin (CIP) as target pollutant. The result implied that different photocatalytic activities for CIP degradation is mainly attributed to different morphologies and microstructures of BiPO4 samples prepared in different solvents. The BiPO4 sample which was prepared under H2O condition showed the highest photocatalytic activity. In addition, inorganic salt NaH2PO4·2H2O was utilized to prepare BiPO4 samples instead of [Omim]H2PO4. It can be found that the photocatalytic efficiency of BiPO4 prepared via ionic liquid exhibited higher photocatalytic activity than that of inorganic salt for the degradation of CIP under UV light irradiation.

ce:section.ce:para: As the problem of organic waste pollution become more serious, new types of effective waste water treatment methods have been abidingly improved [1–3]. In the past decades, semiconductor photocatalysts utilized in environmental decontamination of water pollution using solar energy have been developed rapidly and drawn great attentions from material scientists. Titanium dioxide (TiO2) is one of the semiconductor photocatalysts which has been extensively researched. It was developed as a UV light active photocatalyst which could be applied to degrade organic pollutants in the photocatalytic field, owning to its high activation, great chemical stability and low synthesis price [4–7]. However, as a coin has two sides, there are still remaining two major issues in TiO2 photocatalyst, including sunlight inefficient absorption and the low separation efficiency of photogenerated electron–hole pairs [8]. Therefore, considering the deficiency of TiO2, many researches have been carried out to develop other novel efficient photocatalysts for the development of photocatalytic degradation. Among them, bismuth salts as new type of optical materials have been researched to some extent around the world, such as BiVO4 [9], Bi2WO6 [10], BiOX (X=Cl, Br, I) [11–13], etc, for the development of organic pollutants degradation.

Bismuth phosphate (BiPO4) has been researched as one of the important bismuth-containing salts with high photocatalytic activity under UV light irradiation. Studies show that BiPO4 presents more attractive UV light activity than TiO2 [14,15]. It exhibits high activity not only for the degradation of dyes, such as methyl orange (MO), methyl blue(MB) and rhodamine B (RhB), but also for mineralizing the difficultly degradable phenol. It is surely well known that morphologies and microstructures have great influence on the photocatalytic performance [16]. Thus, for practical applications in the field of photocatalysis research, it is vital to have desired morphologies and microstructures. Different physical and chemical methods have been utilized to synthesize BiPO4 nanostructure, such as chemical vapor deposition process [17], solvothermal reaction [18] and sonochemical method [19]. From the methods above, the solvothermal process is one of the most simple and effective methods for preparing BiPO4 nanostructure. This method could control the morphologies and structures of products via simple routes such as variation of reaction time and temperature, solvents and surfactants. Using a different solvent system in this method contributes to produce the controlled morphology and phase of the nanoparticles, as well as affect the characteristics of the products [20–22]. In the previous reports, a large amount of photocatalysts such as Bi2WO6 [23], ZnO [24], and CdS [20] have been systhesized via a hydrothermal process in different solvents, thus the morphologies and structures of products could be controlled. Nevertheless, the effects of morphologies and structures on photocatalytic performance of BiPO4 by using different solvents have not been reported so far.

Ionic liquids (ILs) are salts in which the ions are poorly coordinated, so that these solvents could be liquid below 100°C, or even at room temperature (room temperature ionic liquids, RTIL’s). Over the time, ionic liquids have been specially developed for the treatment of particular synthetic problems which were known as “designer solvents”. According to our previous work, we have synthesized BiOX (X=Cl, Br, I) samples with different morphologies via reactable ionic liquid [25–28]. It turned out that the ionic liquid played the roles of solvent, reactant and template simultaneously. Inspired by this, [Omim]H2PO4 ionic liquid has been designed for the synthesis of BiPO4 in this system. Up to now, ionic liquid acted as the source of phosphate to synthesis BiPO4 via a facile hydrothermal process has never been reported.

In this work, we prepared the BiPO4 samples with different morphologies via ionic liquid assisted solvothermal process under different solvents such as H2O, ethanol, ethylene glycol (EG) and ethylene glycol monomethyl ether (EGME), respectively. In addition, inorganic salt NaH2PO4·2H2O was utilized to prepare BiPO4 samples instead of [Omim]H2PO4 for comparison. The photocatalytic ability of the as-prepared photocatalysts was evaluated using ciprofloxacin (CIP) as a target pollutant, since the CIP may cause various negative effects on ecosystem by introducing proliferation of bacterial drug resistance via toxicity tests and has brought great human health concerns in natural environments. The relationship between the structure of the photocatalyst and the photocatalytic activities was also discussed in details.
All chemicals were analytical grade and used without further purification. The ionic liquid [Omim]H2PO4 was purchased from Shanghai Chengjie Chemical Co., Ltd.

BiPO4 was synthesized through a typical solvothermal method. In the experimental process, firstly, 3.5mmol [Omim]H2PO4 were dissolved in 20ml four different solutions: H2O, ethanol, EG and EGME, respectively, and were stirred until it became pellucid. Secondly, 1mmol of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was added into the four solvents, respectively. All the mixture were transferred into 25ml Tenflon-lined autoclave after stirring for 30min at room temperature. The uniform and deliquescent solution was heated up to 160°C for 24h in the autoclave and then naturally cooled down to room temperature. Finally the four resulting samples were separated by centrifugation at high-speed about 13,000rpm and washed with deionized water and dehydrated alcohol four times, then dried at 50°C in air.
For the purpose of comparison, BiPO4 samples were also prepared with the method above using NaH2PO4·2H2O as the PO4 − source instead of [Omim]H2PO4 ionic liquid.

The structures and compositions of the as-prepared samples were analyzed by the means of X-ray diffraction XRD, D8 Advance diffractometer with Cu Kα(λ=1.5418nm radiation at 40KV and 100mA) with a scanning speed of 7°min−1 from 10° to 80°. The morphologies and conformation of the products were investigated through the field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, JEOL, JSM-7001F), equipped with an energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) at an acceleration voltage of 10KV. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrographs were taken with a JEOL-JEM-2010 (JEOL, Japan) operating at 200KV. The identification and analysis of molecular structure of the samples were characterized by using KBr disks at room temperature on a Nicolet FT-IR spectrophotometer (Nexus 470, Thermo Electron Corporation). UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) collect the optical diffuse reflectance spectra of samples by using BiSO4 as a white standard reference.

The photocatalytic activities of the BiPO4 samples were measured by the degradation of CIP under UV light irradiation. 30mg of BiPO4 sample was added into 100ml of CIP (10mg/L) solutions. The samples and organic dye were added into in a quartz reactor irradiated with 250W high pressure Hg lamp. In order to ensure that there is plenty of oxygen and can be able to fully promote the mixing of photocatalysts and solution, aeration and stirring were maintained during the photocatalytic reaction process. The suspension was magnetically stirred for 30min in dark to reach adsorption–desorption equilibrium before UV light irradiation. At a certain period of time, a small quantity of suspension (about 3ml) was drawn out from the reaction cell, and was separated by centrifuge at the speed of 13,000rpm for 3min to remove the catalyst inside. The concentration of CIP was determined through an UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, UV-2450) at 276nm.

The XRD was carried out to determine the crystalline structure and phase of the as-prepared products. Fig. 1 depicted the XRD pattern of BiPO4 synthesized in H2O, ethanol, EG and EGME, respectively. All the diffraction patterns can be indexed to the monoclinic phase BiPO4 which consistent with the standard pattern (JCPDS No. 00-015-0767). The main peak positions were marked as (011), (−111), (200), (120), (012) and (−202) planes of BiPO4 material. The strong intensity and sharp peaks of BiPO4 materials indicate that the as-prepared crystals are well crystallized. No other diffraction peaks were discovered, reflecting that pure BiPO4 material can be formed in those four solvents via a solvothermal process.

The FT-IR spectrum of BiPO4 products synthesized in H2O, ethanol, EG and EGME were shown in Fig. S1. The phosphate anion (PO4 3−) possesses two vibration regions (v3 and v4) and C1 symmetry in the monoclinic phase of the prepared BiPO4 [29]. There are four characteristic absorption spectrums at 1073cm−1, 1010cm−1, 953cm−1 and 925cm−1 in the range of 1000cm−1 correspond to the v3 vibration region, which belongs to the PO stretching vibration. The peaks in the vicinity of 1620cm−1 are assigned to the OH groups stretching vibration and bending vibration, owning to the different solvents adsorbed on the surface of BiPO4 products. In addition, the 1380cm−1 is also belongs to the OH stretching vibration. Characteristic peaks at 600cm−1, 535cm−1 and 579cm−1 are assigned to v4 vibration region, which correspond to the δ(OPO) and OPO bending vibration. The result of FT-IR analysis was consistent with the XRD analysis.

The morphologies and microstructures of the pure BiPO4 products synthesized in four different solvents were examined by SEM. Fig. S2 shows the general morphology of the as-prepared samples by field-emission scan electron microscopy (FE-SEM). In the H2O solvent, large numbers of nanorods with smooth surfaces and clear edges were observed (Fig. S2a and b). As the solvents were ethanol, EG, EGME, the morphologies of the BiPO4 samples were transformed from nanorods to nanoparticles which indicated that the solvents play an important role in the size and morphologies of BiPO4 samples. The products were further revealed by TEM, as depicted in Fig. 2 . The nanorods/nanoparticles are clearly observed, which are in accordance with the results of SEM analysis. The BiPO4 samples synthesized in H2O were composed of rod-like nanostructures with the average length about 300nm. Interestingly, under the organic solvent, the sizes of the obtained BiPO4 nanostructures grow smaller and have the similar morphologies, which are different from the morphologies of products obtained under H2O. The diameter of the product is about 30nm, 40nm and 80nm for BiPO4 synthesized in the ethanol, EG and EGME medium, respectively. The above results manifest that the size and morphologies of BiPO4 nanostructures strongly depend on the solvent. As is known that ionic liquid based on 1-alkyl-3methylimidazolium cations could show aggregation behavior and form micelles in solutions [30–33]. For example, James’s research has studied that the 1-octyl-3-methylimidazolium iodide ([C8mim]I) solutions can be modeled as a system of regularly sized near-spherical charged micelles that form a critical micelle concentration [31]. Our previous works also proved that ionic liquids could form different kinds of micelles in different solution, which could result in different shape of materials [27,34–36]. In this work, the ionic liquid could couple with different solvents to form different micelles. The different micelles controlled the nucleate and growth of BiPO4 nanocrystal and thus the BiPO4 materials with different morphologies was formed.

The chemical composition of product prepared in H2O was also confirmed by EDS analysis (Fig. 2h). Except for Si element, bismuth, phosphorus and oxygen elements were discovered in this spectrum with the ratio approximately 1:1:4 (Bi: P: O), which corresponds to the stoichiometries of BiPO4 crystal. The Si element comes from the Si foil. The EDS further confirmed the as-synthesized products were pure BiPO4.

The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of the BiPO4 which prepared under EG, H2O, EGME and ethanol were shown in Fig. 3 a. By investigating the influence of their morphologies and sizes on optical absorption, it is found that all the samples displayed effective photoabsorption at wavelength lower than 280nm, indicating that the BiPO4 has a wide-band-gap and is suitable for photocatalytic decomposition of organic dyes under UV light irradiation. The absorption edge of BiPO4 corresponded well to its band-gap transition. The optical absorption of semiconductor materials is close to the band gap edge abides by the following formula, α hν=A(hν− E g) n/2, where α is absorption coefficient, h is plank constant, νis light frequency, A is proportional constant, and E g is band gap energy [34]. Among them, n is used for determine the type of the optical transition (n =1 represents direct transition and n =4 represents indirect transition). The value of n in BiPO4 is 4, owning to the BiPO4 which shows the characteristic of indirect transition [37]. As shown in Fig. 3b, The band gaps of BiPO4–H2O, BiPO4–EGME, BiPO4–ethanol, BiPO4–EG are 4.27, 4.14, 4.03 and 3.98eV, respectively. The BiPO4 sample prepared in H2O shows the largest band gap.

Nitrogen adsorption–desorption measurements were conducted to investigate the specific surface areas of as-prepared BiPO4 samples. The surface areas of BiPO4 products synthesized in H2O, ethanol, EG and EGME were measured to be 9.16m2 g−1, 24.21m2 g−1, 26.24m2 g−1, 20.64m2 g−1,respectively. BiPO4-H2O has the smallest surface area, which was consistent with the SEM and TEM analysis that the BiPO4 samples with smaller size has larger surface areas.

The photocatalytic activity of the BiPO4 materials was investigated for the degradation of ciprofloxacin (CIP). It would be the first report by using the BiPO4 for the CIP photodegradation as far as concern, and the results are shown in Fig. 4 . Simple CIP photolysis was also performed for comparison. As Fig. 4(a and b) shown that the blank experiment with the absence of photocatalysts demonstrates that slight CIP was photodegraded by the UV light irradiation, which is approximately 4.9%. When the BiPO4 materials were added, the CIP can be effective photodegraded under the UV light irradiation. For the BiPO4 nanomaterials, adsorption of CIP occurred initially and reached saturation in less than 30min. The final adsorption capacity of CIP is shown in Table S1. And no further change in the concentration of CIP until the UV light was turned on. The concentration of CIP then decreased quickly with increasing irradiation time. From Fig. 4a, among the BiPO4 materials which prepared in different solvents, the BiPO4–H2O material exhibits the highest photocatalytic activity. 99.9% CIP can be degraded by BiPO4–H2O material after irradiation for 90min. When the solvents are changed from H2O to EGME, ethanol and EG, the degradation of CIP shows the tendency of decreasing. The degradation of CIP is 97.1%, 91.6% and 90.1% for BiPO4–EGME, ethanol and EG, respectively. The photocatalytic activities of TiO2 (Degussa, P25) were measured on the degradation of CIP under the UV light irradiation. After irradiation for 90min, 92% of CIP was photodegraded by Degussa P25, while 99% and 97% of CIP were photodegraded by BiPO4–H2O, BiPO4–EGME, respectively. The results assumed that BiPO4–H2O, BiPO4–EGME samples showed higher photocatalytic activity than the conventional method prepared sample and TiO2 (Degussa, P25). For comparison, the BiPO4 materials were synthetized by using inorganic salt NaH2PO4·2H2O as react source instead of [Omim]H2PO4. From the Fig. 4b, the degradation of CIP is 70.4%, 54.4%, 49.1%, 47.1%, respectively, for BiPO4–H2O, EGME, ethanol and EG. Among them, it was also found that the BiPO4–H2O material possesses the optimum degradation of CIP. What is more, the photocatalytic activity is decreased with the solvents are changed from H2O to EGME, ethanol and EG. The results are similar to that in Fig. 4a. The photodegradation experiment shows that the BiPO4 materials prepared via NaH2PO4·2H2O display lower photocatalytic activity than ionic liquid assisted synthetized BiPO4 materials. Besides, the chemical oxygen demand (COD) removal efficiencies of the CIP by BiPO4–H2O, BiPO4–Ethanol, BiPO4–EG and BiPO4–EGME materials prepared via ionic liquid under ultraviolet light irradiation are shown in Fig. 5 . COD value of BiPO4–H2O, EGME, ethanol and EG is 88.01, 90.32, 118.19 and 120.10mg/L, respectively. The initial COD value of CIP is about 174.20mg/L. And COD removal is 49.48%, 48.15%, 32.15% and 31.06%, respectively. Obviously, the BiPO4–H2O material exhibits the highest COD removal and thus the highest photocatalytic activity. The dramatic activity reveals that BiPO4 material synthetized via ionic liquid is an efficient photocatalyst and may become valuable photocatalytic material in potential applications for environmental protection.

To understand the different photocatalytic activity of the BiPO4 material prepared under different solvent, several factors such as morphology, BET surface area and energy band structure have been addressed. Firstly, it is well known that the photocatalytic activity is closely related to the adsorption ability and surface area of the catalyst. The higher surface area of the catalyst may result in the enhanced photocatalytic activity [35]. However, the BiPO4–H2O material, which shows the highest photocatalytic activity, has the smallest surface area. Secondly, light absorption and energy band structure plays an important role on the photocatalytic activity. The higher light absorption and a lower band gap favor the electronic transitions and could excite easier production of active species, which may subsequently result in an enhanced photocatalytic activity [36]. However, the BiPO4–H2O material has the largest band gap in this system. Thus, the different photocatalytic activity of BiPO4 material can be reasonable attributed to the different morphologies and surface microstructures.

BiPO4 photocatalyst with various morphologies and microstructures were synthesized via a facile solvothermal process in H2O, ethanol, EG and EGME, respectively. It could be seen that the type of solvents have great effect on the morphologies and sizes of BiPO4. Furthermore, compared to the inorganic salt NaH2PO4 ·2H2O, the ionic liquid [Omim]H2PO4 as the source of phosphas plays significant roles in preparing the high-efficient BiPO4 photocatalysts. The different photocatalytic activity of BiPO4 material can be reasonable attributed to the different morphologies and surface microstructures. The resulting BiPO4 materials prepared using ionic liquid are shown as very promising photocatalysts for degrading organic pollutants while the synthesis strategy by using reactable ionic liquid can be extended to fabricate other materials.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Nature Science Foundation of China (No. 21206060, 21471069 and 21476098) and the Special Financial Grant from the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2013T60506).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfa.2015.09.061.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:
